note_data.title: Making measurements
keyPoints: **Length** is measured using rulers, metre rules, measuring tapes, and callipers for accurate measurements
**Volume** of regular solids is calculated using length × width × height; irregular solids use displacement method
**Density** is the mass per unit volume of a substance, measured in kg/m³ or g/cm³
**Time** is measured using stopwatches, with repeated measurements taken to reduce random errors
**Parallax error** occurs when the eye is not positioned directly above the scale reading
**Systematic errors** cause all readings to be consistently higher or lower than the true value
**Random errors** cause readings to scatter around the true value and can be reduced by taking repeat readings
**Precision** refers to how close repeated measurements are to each other
**Accuracy** refers to how close a measurement is to the true value

definitions.definition: The mass per unit volume of a substance
The error that occurs when the eye is not positioned directly above the scale when taking a reading
An error that causes all readings to differ from the true value by the same amount
An error that causes readings to scatter randomly around the true value

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give a step-by-step method or detailed account of the process
Show working clearly with correct formula, substitution and final answer with units

modelAnswer.question: A student measures the density of an irregular stone. Describe how the student can find the volume of the stone using the displacement method.
modelAnswer.answer: 1. Fill a measuring cylinder with water and record the initial volume. 2. Carefully lower the stone into the water using a thread to avoid splashing. 3. Record the new water level. 4. The volume of the stone equals the difference between the final and initial water levels.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Fill measuring cylinder with water and record initial volume
Mark 2: Lower stone into water carefully
Mark 4: Volume of stone = final volume - initial volume

commonMistakes: Never say 'weight per unit volume' for density — always say 'mass per unit volume'
Students often forget to include units in final answers for calculations
When measuring time, students forget that human reaction time causes errors — always take repeat readings
Confusing accuracy with precision — accuracy is closeness to true value, precision is repeatability
Not positioning eye at right angles to scale causes parallax error
examinerTips: Measurement questions appear in almost every paper — especially density calculations
Always show working for calculations: formula, substitution, answer with units
For error questions, identify the type of error AND suggest how to reduce it
Remember to convert units correctly (cm³ to m³, g to kg) in density problems
formulas: volume of cuboid = length × width × height
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Measuring cylinder showing water displacement method: initial water level, stone being lowered in, and final water level marked clearly with volume readings
